Blind Neuron-Model Recovery — a Generalized Integrate-and-Fire equation for V(t)

Author

Asta Research Challenge

Published

July 16, 2026

1 Summary

Given only the injected current I(t) and the recorded membrane potential V(t) of a single neuron (four stimulus conditions differing in current bandwidth, 25/100/200/400 Hz), I recovered an explicit equation for V(t) and validated it on held-out trials. The data point unambiguously to a Generalized Integrate-and-Fire (GIF / GLIF) model (Gerstner et al. 2014; Mensi et al. 2012; Teeter et al. 2018): a linear leaky membrane driven by I(t), plus a spike-triggered adaptation current, a spike-triggered (dynamic) threshold, and a reset. One parameter set fits all four conditions.

On a held-out split of the training trials (20 %, never used for fitting), the model reproduces the subthreshold voltage with RMSE ≈ 2.5 mV (subthreshold R² ≈ 0.87) and predicts spike times with a Victor–Purpura distance (q = 4 s⁻¹) of ≈ 2.9 per second and a coincidence factor Γ ≈ 0.69 (±2 ms). Predicted and measured firing rates agree to within a few Hz in every condition.

2 1. The equation

Subthreshold membrane — a single-compartment leaky integrator driven by the injected current, with a spike-triggered adaptation current \(I_\text{adapt}\):

\[ C\,\frac{dV}{dt} \;=\; -\,g_L\,(V - E_L)\;+\;I(t)\;-\;I_\text{adapt}(t), \qquad I_\text{adapt}(t)=\sum_{k} w_k\, z_k(t). \]

Each adaptation state \(z_k\) is a leaky integrator of the spike train that decays with time constant \(\tau_k\) and jumps by 1 at every spike time \(\hat t\):

\[ \tau_k \frac{dz_k}{dt} = -z_k,\qquad z_k \to z_k+1 \ \text{at each spike} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad z_k(t)=\sum_{\hat t < t} e^{-(t-\hat t)/\tau_k}. \]

Spike generation with a dynamic threshold — a spike is emitted at time \(\hat t\) when the voltage reaches a threshold that itself rises after each spike (accommodation / refractoriness):

\[ \text{spike when}\quad V(t)\ge V_T(t), \qquad V_T(t)=V_{T0}+a_\theta\,\theta(t), \qquad \tau_\theta \frac{d\theta}{dt}=-\theta,\ \ \theta\to\theta+1 \ \text{at each spike}. \]

Reset / refractoriness — immediately after a spike, \(V\to V_r\) and is held for a refractory period \(t_\text{ref}\); the states \(z_k\) and \(\theta\) each increment by 1.

For visualization/scoring, the returned trace pastes a stereotyped action-potential waveform (the training spike-triggered average) at each spike time; this affects only the ~3 ms around each spike.

Symbols. \(V\) membrane potential (mV); \(I(t)\) injected current (pA); \(C\) capacitance (pF); \(g_L\) leak conductance (nS), \(R=1/g_L\); \(E_L\) leak/resting potential (mV); \(\tau_m=C/g_L\) membrane time constant; \(w_k,\tau_k\) amplitudes/time-constants of the adaptation current (pA, ms); \(V_{T0}\) baseline threshold (mV); \(a_\theta,\tau_\theta\) threshold jump/decay (mV, ms); \(V_r\) reset (mV); \(t_\text{ref}\) refractory period (ms).

This is the GIF model of Mensi et al. (2012) and Pozzorini et al. (2015), i.e. an Allen-Institute-style GLIF with a spike-triggered current and an adaptive threshold (Teeter et al. 2018).

3 2. Fitted parameters (this cell)

quantity symbol value unit
capacitance \(C\) 66.3 pF
leak conductance \(g_L\) 9.88 nS
input resistance \(R=1/g_L\) 101 MΩ
membrane time constant \(\tau_m=C/g_L\) 6.71 ms
leak / resting potential \(E_L\) −65.2 mV
baseline threshold \(V_{T0}\) −55.3 mV
threshold jump \(a_\theta\) 4.12 mV
threshold time constant \(\tau_\theta\) 30.5 ms
reset potential \(V_r\) −61.8 mV
refractory period \(t_\text{ref}\) 3.45 ms

Spike-triggered adaptation current \(I_\text{adapt}(t)=\sum_k w_k e^{-t/\tau_k}\) (per spike):

\(\tau_k\) (ms) 3 10 30 100 300
\(w_k\) (pA) 35.0 −11.9 23.3 −3.4 0.22

The net \(I_\text{adapt}\) is a hyperpolarising current of ≈ 47 pA immediately after a spike that decays over tens of ms (see figure below) — a spike-frequency-adaptation / after-hyperpolarisation current. Values are physiologically typical for a cortical neuron.

4 3. How the equation was found (data → model)

I did not assume the model form; three model-free views of the data selected it (work/explore-data/):

  1. Dynamic I–V curve (Badel et al. 2008). Plotting the net ionic current \(I-C\,\dot V\) against \(V\) over subthreshold samples gives a straight line that crosses zero at \(E_L\approx-65\) mV for \(V\lesssim-50\) mV, then turns sharply inward above ≈ −55 mV. A straight subthreshold I–V is a linear leak; the sharp upturn is the regenerative (Na⁺) spike current. → leaky membrane + a voltage threshold.
  2. Phase plot (\(\dot V\) vs \(V\)). A sharp spike-initiation “elbow” near −55 mV and a stereotyped AP loop → threshold + reset + a fixed AP waveform, rather than a continuous nonlinearity.
  3. Spike-triggered average. A reset to ≈ −60 mV followed by a slow after-hyperpolarisation and spike-frequency adaptation → a spike-triggered adaptation current \(I_\text{adapt}\) and a moving threshold.

The passive parameters were consistent across all four bandwidth conditions (per-condition \(\tau_m\) = 6–10 ms, \(C\) = 62–90 pF, \(E_L\) ≈ −65 mV), confirming a single cell and justifying one shared equation. An independent DataVoyager pass on the same CSVs reproduced these passive estimates (e.g. \(\tau_m\) = 8.4 / 6.0 / 6.6 / 9.9 ms, \(C\) = 86 / 73 / 72 / 63 pF), corroborating the fit.

4.1 Fitting method

  • Membrane + adaptation current by linear regression: over subthreshold samples (excluding a 4 ms window around each spike and \(V>-40\) mV), regress \(\dot V\) on \([\,V,\,1,\,I,\,z_1..z_5\,]\). The coefficients give \(C,g_L,E_L\) and the adaptation weights \(w_k\) in one closed-form step (the Badel/Mensi/Pozzorini estimator (Badel et al. 2008; Pozzorini et al. 2015)). No forward simulation is needed for this part, so it is robust.
  • Spike-generation parameters (\(V_{T0},a_\theta,\tau_\theta,V_r,t_\text{ref}\), and an overall adaptation scale) by direct search (Nelder–Mead, multi-start) that minimises the Victor–Purpura spike distance (\(q=4\,\mathrm{s^{-1}}\)) between model and data, pooled across the four conditions.
  • Spikes are detected with an elbow (v2) method: the AP-initiation point is the “kink” where the Na⁺ current engages, located as the maximum of the second derivative \(d^2V/dt^2\) on the rising phase (Sekerli et al. 2004). The identical detector is applied to measured and predicted traces when scoring.

5 4. Held-out performance

Metrics are computed on a 20 % held-out split of the training trials (random, seed-fixed, never used for fitting), per condition. VP is the Victor–Purpura distance per second; Γ is the coincidence factor at ±2 ms (Jolivet et al. 2008); subthreshold RMSE/R² exclude a window around every spike.

condition VP dist / s (q=4) Γ (±2 ms) subthr. RMSE (mV) subthr. R² rate meas/pred (Hz)
25 Hz 2.63 0.642 2.23 0.875 25.0 / 24.1
100 Hz 4.45 0.564 3.11 0.860 41.4 / 45.4
200 Hz 2.37 0.807 1.94 0.886 34.3 / 34.3
400 Hz 2.21 0.751 2.64 0.870 40.0 / 40.3
mean 2.91 0.691 2.48 0.873 —

The held-out predictions for the mission’s input-only files are written to predictions/pred_cond{25,100,200,400}Hz.csv.gz (trial_id, t_s, voltage_mV, one row per input sample) by python model/predict.py.

6 5. Where it fails / limitations

  • 100 Hz is the weakest condition (VP/s = 4.4, Γ = 0.56): the model slightly over-fires (41→45 Hz). This condition has the largest mean holding current and the model’s fixed reset makes it a touch too excitable in sustained depolarisations.
  • Exact spike timing cannot be recovered to <1–2 ms: near threshold a small voltage error moves a spike by several ms, and the trials are frozen-noise-free (every trial is a different current, so there is no repeat-reliability ceiling to approach). This is intrinsic to any deterministic threshold model; a stochastic-threshold GIF would trade a reproducible trace for better timing statistics.
  • Startup transient. Each trial is initialised at \(E_L\) (no pre-stimulus history is given for the held-out inputs), so the first ~20–30 ms can show a small relaxation transient.
  • AHP depth on very strong hyperpolarising currents (400 Hz) can overshoot the data by a few mV, because the leak is strictly linear.

7 6. Data vs. prior knowledge

From the data (this cell): the model form (linear leak + threshold + reset + adaptation) was selected from the dynamic I–V curve, phase plot, and spike-triggered average; and all numeric parameters were fit to data/ only. From prior knowledge: the GIF/GLIF framework and its regression fitting protocol (Mensi et al. 2012; Pozzorini et al. 2015; Badel et al. 2008; Teeter et al. 2018), the Victor–Purpura metric (Victor and Purpura 1996), the coincidence factor (Jolivet et al. 2008), and the second-derivative “elbow” spike-onset detector (Sekerli et al. 2004). No external cell/answer was used.

8 7. Reproducing this

python3 -m venv venv && source venv/bin/activate
pip install "numpy<2" "pandas>=2" scipy matplotlib numba   # numba optional (speeds the fit)

python model/glif_fit.py             # refit -> model/params.json, model/heldout_metrics.json
python model/predict.py              # write predictions/pred_cond*.csv.gz
python model/make_report_figures.py  # regenerate figures/

model/predict.py exposes the required predictor: predict_voltage(current_pA) -> voltage_mV for one trial (state reset per trial). model/params.json holds the fitted equation.

References

Badel, Laurent, Sandrine Lefort, Romain Brette, Carl C. H. Petersen, Wulfram Gerstner, and Magnus J. E. Richardson. 2008. “Dynamic i-v Curves Are Reliable Predictors of Naturalistic Pyramidal-Neuron Voltage Traces.” Journal of Neurophysiology 99 (2): 656–66. https://doi.org/10.1152/jn.01107.2007.
Gerstner, Wulfram, Werner M. Kistler, Richard Naud, and Liam Paninski. 2014. Neuronal Dynamics: From Single Neurons to Networks and Models of Cognition. Cambridge University Press.
Jolivet, Renaud, Felix Schürmann, Thomas K. Berger, Richard Naud, Wulfram Gerstner, and Arnd Roth. 2008. “A Benchmark Test for a Quantitative Assessment of Simple Neuron Models.” Journal of Neuroscience Methods 169 (2): 417–24. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jneumeth.2007.11.006.
Mensi, Skander, Richard Naud, Christian Pozzorini, Michael Avermann, Carl C. H. Petersen, and Wulfram Gerstner. 2012. “Parameter Extraction and Classification of Three Cortical Neuron Types Reveals Two Distinct Adaptation Mechanisms.” Journal of Neurophysiology 107 (6): 1756–75. https://doi.org/10.1152/jn.00408.2011.
Pozzorini, Christian, Skander Mensi, Olivier Hagens, Richard Naud, Christof Koch, and Wulfram Gerstner. 2015. “Automated High-Throughput Characterization of Single Neurons by Means of Simplified Spiking Models.” PLoS Computational Biology 11 (6): e1004275. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pcbi.1004275.
Sekerli, Mustafa, Christopher A. Del Negro, Robert H. Lee, and Robert J. Butera. 2004. “Estimating Action Potential Thresholds from Neuronal Time-Series: New Metrics and Evaluation of Methodologies.” IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Engineering 51 (9): 1665–72. https://doi.org/10.1109/TBME.2004.827531.
Teeter, Corinne, Ramakrishnan Iyer, Vilas Menon, Nathan Gouwens, et al. 2018. “Generalized Leaky Integrate-and-Fire Models Classify Multiple Neuron Types.” Nature Communications 9: 709. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-017-02717-4.
Victor, Jonathan D., and Keith P. Purpura. 1996. “Nature and Precision of Temporal Coding in Visual Cortex: A Metric-Space Analysis.” Journal of Neurophysiology 76 (2): 1310–26. https://doi.org/10.1152/jn.1996.76.2.1310.
